one_line_takeaway: A very large (~900-reference, 69-page) survey that introduces a 'full-stack' taxonomy organizing LLM and LLM-agent safety across the entire lifecycle (data, pre-training, post-training, deployment, commercialization) under a uniform attack/defense/evaluation lens, claiming to be the first to span all stages.
key_finding: The synthesized takeaway is that LLM safety is multifaceted and stage-coupled: vulnerabilities compound across the lifecycle (toxic/poisoned data seeps into pre-trained parameters, misalignment and harmful fine-tuning degrade safety post-training, and deployment/agent modules add jailbreak and tool/memory contamination surfaces), so single-stage surveys and defenses are structurally incomplete. The paper argues a lifecycle-wide ('full-stack') view is needed and highlights data generation safety, alignment techniques, model editing/unlearning, and LLM-agent security as the highest-leverage future directions.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical paper). Its central contribution is the concept and taxonomy of 'full-stack' LLM(-agent) safety: a single framework that organizes safety research across the ENTIRE LLM lifecycle rather than a single stage. The authors partition the lifecycle into data preparation (Sec. 2), pre-training (Sec. 3), post-training split into alignment and fine-tuning plus model editing and unlearning (Sec. 4-5), deployment covering both pure LLMs and LLM-based agents with tools/memory (Sec. 6), and commercialization/ethics/usage (Sec. 7), followed by future directions (Sec. 8) and conclusion (Sec. 9). Within each stage the survey uses a consistent attack / defense / evaluation structure and ends each chapter with a 'roadmap and perspective.' The paper explicitly frames this as the first safety survey to span the complete lifecycle, and Table 1 positions it against ~24 prior surveys, claiming it is the only one covering all of Data, Pre-training, Editing, Fine-tuning, Deployment, and Evaluation for single-modal + multi-modal LLMs and single/multi-modal/multi-agent systems.
threat_model: Because this is a survey, it does not defend a single threat model; instead it catalogs the threat landscape stage-by-stage. Threats it organizes include: training-data poisoning and privacy leakage during data collection and pre-training; toxic/biased data absorbed by unsupervised pre-training; harmful fine-tuning attacks and misalignment during post-training; knowledge-conflict and stale-parameter risks addressed by model editing/unlearning (and their misuse to strip safety guardrails); jailbreak attacks and defenses at deployment; and agent-specific contamination via tools, memory, and environment interaction in single- and multi-agent systems. Adversaries range from data-supply-chain attackers to prompt-level jailbreakers to compromised agent tool/memory channels.
limitations: As a survey it contributes organization and synthesis rather than new evidence: no empirical evaluation, no reproduced results, and reported numbers are inherited from cited works without independent verification. Breadth-over-depth is inevitable given ~900 papers across the whole lifecycle. The authors themselves note substantial overlap between model editing and unlearning that they separate only for readability, indicating taxonomy boundaries are partly presentational. A fast-moving field means the coverage (v4, June 2025) will date quickly, and the self-comparison in Table 1 (claiming most-complete coverage) is authored by the surveyors themselves.

quantitative_results: Coverage claim: exhaustive review of 'over 900+ papers' (stated in abstract and conclusion); Contribution 2 states 'over 800 pieces of literature'.

Table 1 compares the survey against ~24 prior LLM/agent surveys (2023-2025); the 'Ours' row is the only one checking all six lifecycle columns: Data, Pre-training, Editing, Fine-tuning, Deployment, Evaluation.

Illustrative surveyed finding (cited from primary works [84],[85], not the survey's own experiment): as little as 0.1% poisoned pre-training data can have lasting impact on model behavior even after extensive fine-tuning.
Illustrative surveyed finding (Sun et al. [81], cited): renaming a single variable/function name (code poisoning) can push vulnerable code into the top 11% of code-search rankings.
Document scope: 69 pages, bibliography exceeding ~919 entries.
baselines_compared: Not an experimental paper, so no model baselines. The closest comparison is Table 1's feature-level positioning against prior surveys, e.g. Zhao et al. [6], Chang et al. [7], Huang et al. [51], Ma et al. [33], Huang et al. [73], Yu et al. [74], Chen et al. [36], which each cover only subsets of the lifecycle.
